summary: Stancil argues that the data industry has inflated the word “insight” into a vague marketing promise, even though most users and businesses mainly want reliable, basic reporting about what’s happening. He contends that data teams chase flashy analysis because it is socially and commercially rewarded, while the harder, more valuable work of accurate reporting is undervalued.
money_quotes: Insights are grandiose enough to sound valuable, and amorphous enough to avoid actually saying what that value is.
The real insights, the ones that people are paying for and are consistently valuable, are simple descriptions of fact.
More often than not, this isn’t insight; it’s distortion.
Until we celebrate and reward the “simple” work of creating basic reports and of working with experts around the business, data teams and data vendors will keep chasing the false idol of insight.
key_insight: The industry’s obsession with “insight” obscures the real value of data work, which is delivering clear, accurate reporting about what’s happening.
